content:
You’ve Never Heard of Triangular Roots.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by the AuthorIf this is your first time coming across the term “triangular roots,” then you are where I was six months ago.
Geometry and algebra are different branches of mathematics, but the way they complement each other has always fascinated me. Triangular roots are a perfect example of this connection.
When objects can be arranged to form a square, their total is called a square number. The same idea applies to triangular numbers — a number is called triangular when that many objects can be arranged in rows to form a triangle.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Triangular Number — The count of all the black circles gives 6, while the count of all the green balls gives 10 when each set is arranged in a triangular pattern.1,3, 6, 10,15, 21, 28, ... are considered triangular numbers.
Just like square numbers have roots, triangular numbers also have roots.
If you arrange the dots in a square pattern, the height or width of the square pattern tells you the root of that square. This also holds for triangular numbers.
So, the triangular root of 10 would be 4.
